perturbation.original_text: 1. "Confidential Information" means all information provided by either Party in writing or visual form which Party desires to protect from disclosure and which is marked with an appropriate stamp or legend designating such material as "Confidential"; and all information provided orally by a Party which that Party desires to protect from disclosure provided that such information is identified as Confidential at the time it is transmitted and is subsequently confirmed as such in writing by the disclosing Party within fifteen (15) days after such verbal transmittal.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. "Confidential Information" means all information provided by either Party in writing or visual form which Party desires to protect from disclosure and which is marked with an appropriate stamp or legend designating such material as "Confidential"; and all information provided orally by a Party which that Party desires to protect from disclosure provided that such information is identified as Confidential at the time it is transmitted.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement for oral disclosures to be subsequently confirmed in writing within fifteen days. This creates a contradiction because later sections (specifically section 7) refer to the comprehensive definition, implying the written confirmation is necessary for all oral disclosures to be treated as confidential. Section 1 is now internally contradicting due to the change.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Neither Party shall disclose Confidential Information nor assign work hereunder to anyone other than the receiving Party's employees who have agreed in writing to maintain the Confidential Information as confidential and to execute all documents deemed reasonably necessary by the disclosing Party to permit compliance with all provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change removes the requirement for written confirmation of oral disclosures to be considered confidential. Section 7 requires written agreements to maintain confidentiality, which implicitly relies on the original definition of 'Confidential Information' that includes this written confirmation for oral disclosures. The modified Section 1 now contradicts Section 7 because oral information can be confidential without written confirmation according to the modified Section 1, which conflicts with Section 7's confidentiality requirements.

perturbation.original_text: 2. For a period of five (5) years following the date of each such disclosure or development the receiving Party shall maintain such Confidential Information as confidential, except: (a) Confidential Information which is or becomes known publicly through no fault of the receiving Party; or (b) Confidential Information learned by the receiving Party from a third party entitled to disclose it; or (c) Confidential Information already known to the receiving Party before receipt from the disclosing Party as shown by the receiving Party's written records; or (d) Confidential Information which is independently developed by the receiving Party as shown by the receiving Party's written records; or (e) Confidential Information which must be disclosed under operation of law. (f) Confidential Information which is approved for release by written authorization of the disclosing Party
perturbation.changed_text: 2. For a period of two (2) years following the date of each such disclosure or development the receiving Party shall maintain such Confidential Information as confidential, except: (a) Confidential Information which is or becomes known publicly through no fault of the receiving Party; or (b) Confidential Information learned by the receiving Party from a third party entitled to disclose it; or (c) Confidential Information already known to the receiving Party before receipt from the disclosing Party as shown by the receiving Party's written records; or (d) Confidential Information which is independently developed by the receiving Party as shown by the receiving Party's written records; or (e) Confidential Information which must be disclosed under operation of law. (f) Confidential Information which is approved for release by written authorization of the disclosing Party
perturbation.explanation: This change shortens the confidentiality period from five years to two years. This contradicts the general statement on disclosing confidential information in Section 3, creating uncertainty. The in-text contradiction is that section 2 states a time period of two years, but section 3 could be interpreted to imply a longer period if 'Confidential Information must be maintained confidential' indefinitely.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. The receiving Party shall not, without prior written permission of the disclosing Party, furnish to any third Party any information, equipment or material embodying or made by use of any Confidential Information <PAGE> -7- received or developed hereunder nor use such information for purposes other than internal evaluation so long as such Confidential Information must be maintained confidential. All Confidential Information in tangible form, and any copies thereof, disclosed hereunder, shall be promptly returned to the disclosing Party at the disclosing Party's written request, or destroyed at the disclosing Parties request provided that confirmation of destruction is given to the disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the confidentiality period from five years to two years in Section 2. The contradicted text in Section 3 states that confidential information must be maintained confidential "so long as such Confidential Information must be maintained confidential." The original text with the five-year period aligned reasonably well with the indefinite confidentiality period implied by Section 3, even if there was a minor ambiguity. However, shortening the period to two years creates a direct and clear contradiction: Section 2 imposes a two-year limit, while Section 3 could be interpreted to require confidentiality beyond that period. This creates a strong in-text contradiction, making the contract unclear and potentially unenforceable because it's uncertain how long the receiving party must maintain confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: 3. The receiving Party shall not, without prior written permission of the disclosing Party, furnish to any third Party any information, equipment or material embodying or made by use of any Confidential Information <PAGE> -7- received or developed hereunder nor use such information for purposes other than internal evaluation so long as such Confidential Information must be maintained confidential. All Confidential Information in tangible form, and any copies thereof, disclosed hereunder, shall be promptly returned to the disclosing Party at the disclosing Party's written request, or destroyed at the disclosing Parties request provided that confirmation of destruction is given to the disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The receiving Party may, without prior written permission of the disclosing Party, furnish to any third Party any information, equipment or material embodying or made by use of any Confidential Information <PAGE> -7- received or developed hereunder and use such information for purposes other than internal evaluation so long as such Confidential Information must be maintained confidential. All Confidential Information in tangible form, and any copies thereof, disclosed hereunder, shall be promptly returned to the disclosing Party at the disclosing Party's written request, or destroyed at the disclosing Parties request provided that confirmation of destruction is given to the disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The word 'not' is removed, now allowing the receiving party to furnish information to a third party. The in-text contradiction occurs with section 4, which states the receiving party must prevent unauthorized use, dissemination or publication of confidential information. With this change, it's contradictory if disseminating to a third party goes against preventing dissemination of the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. The receiving Party shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent the unauthorised use, dissemination or publication of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the word 'not' from Section 3, which originally prohibited the receiving party from furnishing confidential information to a third party. Now, Section 3 allows the receiving party to furnish such information. However, Section 4 requires the receiving party to prevent the unauthorized dissemination of confidential information. Allowing the receiving party to furnish information to a third party directly contradicts the obligation in Section 4 to prevent dissemination, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. In the event of any breach or threatened breach by the receiving Party of its obligations hereunder, the disclosing Party shall have whatever rights and remedies are available to it at law or in equity, provided, however, that in no event shall either Party be liable for any consequential damages of any nature whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. In the event of any breach or threatened breach by the receiving Party of its obligations hereunder, neither Party shall have any rights and remedies are available to it at law or in equity, provided, however, that in no event shall either Party be liable for any consequential damages of any nature whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'the disclosing party shall have whatever rights' to 'neither party shall have any rights', the ability to take legal action in the event of a breach is removed for both parties. This contradicts the implied rights and remedies preserved in Section 8 concerning improving existing products and systems. If a breach occurs concerning information obtained, it is unclear as to whether any party can seek remedy or prevent the improved products and systems.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall constitute a commitment by either Party to the development or release of any future products and/or programs disclosed thereby or restrict either Party in its efforts to improve its existing products and systems and to conceive and develop new products and systems. Additionally, participation in the information exchange pursuant to this Agreement shall not constitute or imply a commitment by either Party to favour or recommend any product or service of the other Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 5 to state that 'neither Party shall have any rights and remedies are available to it at law or in equity' in the event of a breach. This directly contradicts Section 8, which implicitly acknowledges each party's ability to improve existing products and systems. The change effectively removes the legal recourse needed to prevent misuse of confidential information in developing those products and systems. Without rights or remedies, the ability to protect improvements based on disclosed information, a core aspect of Section 8, is negated. This conflict leads to a strong in-text contradiction, making the agreement unenforceable with respect to confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 6. This Agreement shall not be construed as granting or conferring any rights to license or otherwise, expressly, implied, or otherwise for any invention, discovery or improvement made, conceived or acquired prior to or after the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. This Agreement shall be construed as granting or conferring rights to license or otherwise, expressly, implied, or otherwise for any invention, discovery or improvement made, conceived or acquired prior to or after the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'shall' now grants licensing rights, which contradicts Section 7. While section 7 prevents the assignment of work, section 6 contradicts that, since it now implies the confidential information, inventions, discovery, or improvement may be licensed to other parties
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Neither Party shall disclose Confidential Information nor assign work hereunder to anyone other than the receiving Party's employees who have agreed in writing to maintain the Confidential Information as confidential and to execute all documents deemed reasonably necessary by the disclosing Party to permit compliance with all provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 6 explicitly prohibited the granting of licensing rights, whereas the changed text now states that the agreement 'shall be construed as granting or conferring rights to license'. This directly contradicts Section 7, which prevents the assignment of work or disclosure of confidential information to anyone other than the receiving Party's employees. The change in Section 6 now implies that confidential information, inventions, discoveries, or improvements may be licensed to other parties, which conflicts with the confidentiality and non-assignment clauses in Section 7. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists, causing ambiguity and potential unenforceability regarding licensing and confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall constitute a commitment by either Party to the development or release of any future products and/or programs disclosed thereby or restrict either Party in its efforts to improve its existing products and systems and to conceive and develop new products and systems. Additionally, participation in the information exchange pursuant to this Agreement shall not constitute or imply a commitment by either Party to favour or recommend any product or service of the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall constitute a commitment by either Party to the development or release of any future products and/or programs disclosed thereby, and restrict either Party in its efforts to improve its existing products and systems and to conceive and develop new products and systems. Additionally, participation in the information exchange pursuant to this Agreement shall not constitute or imply a commitment by either Party to favour or recommend any product or service of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'or restrict' creates a commitment and restrict either party in its efforts to improve its existing products and systems. This change directly conflicts with Section 3, which allows for the Confidential Information to be returned to the disclosing party, effectively preventing further development or use. The text now creates an internal contradiction, as section 8 now restricts party in its efforts to improve its existing products and systems.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. The receiving Party shall not, without prior written permission of the disclosing Party, furnish to any third Party any information, equipment or material embodying or made by use of any Confidential Information <PAGE> -7- received or developed hereunder nor use such information for purposes other than internal evaluation so long as such Confidential Information must be maintained confidential. All Confidential Information in tangible form, and any copies thereof, disclosed hereunder, shall be promptly returned to the disclosing Party at the disclosing Party's written request, or destroyed at the disclosing Parties request provided that confirmation of destruction is given to the disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that nothing in the agreement restricts either party from improving its existing products, maintaining consistency with Section 3, which allows for the return of confidential information, implying the potential cessation of development. However, the changed text removes 'or restrict', which now introduces a restriction in either party's efforts, directly contradicting Section 3's allowance for the return of confidential information, effectively preventing further development or use.
